Dear Dr. feehan:
Frrr, Potiri"al Pract ices Commissio4
P.O. BOX 807 . SACRAMENTO,

/ il? 1/7
Reference our telephone discussion of october 26, 1975, and
your letter of October 11 , 1976, to Governor Edmund G. Bror.lln, Jr.,
requesting information pertaining to disclosure of income received
from clients by persons serving on public boards.
I attach for your review a copy of the Political Reform Act
of !974. Although this Act has been ameniled at numerous sections
during the recent Legislative Sessi,on, for the purpose of your
questions, the enclosed copy continues to be relevant. r direct
your attention to Chapter 7, Article 2, of Ehe Act (at page 42).
Beginning at Government Code Section E7200, the Act establishes
disal.osure requirements for various high-ranking state and locaL
public officials. 
Many of these persons, such as mer,rcers of city
councils or boards of supervisors, are within the scope of your
question. Pursuant to the provisions of Article 2, those persons
named in Section 87200, are required to file Statements of
Economic Interests. 
The initial 
statements required do not include
income (see Section 87202). The anniversary and leaving office
-statements, 
described in Sections 87203 and 87204, do require
income to be disclosed.
Senate Bill 1356, which becomes effective ,ranuary l, 1977,
modifies the income reporting requirements of the above-re ferenced
sections. Section 82030 (at page 15) defines income. This
definitional Section does not limit reportable j.ncome to the juris-
diction. 
Senate Bill l-356, however, amends this Section to provide
that income does not include income received from -any source outside
the jurisdiction and not doing business within the jurisdicti.on,
not planning to do business within the jurC.sdiction, or not having
done busineis within the jurisdiction during the two years prior
to the time any statement or other action is required under the
Act. Accordingly, the income now required to be disclosed,
pursuant to Sections 87203 and 87204 of Article 2, is limited to
income from within the jurisdiction.
l now direct your attention to Chapter 7, Article 3 (at page
45) of the Act. Many persons serving on public bodies will be




Dr. Edward B. Feehan
November 5, 797 6
required. to discl,ose income, not because of Articl-e 2 disclosure
requirements, but pursuant to a Conflict of Interest Code
adopted by the agency that they serve. Section 87302 (b) of
Article 3, makes income reportable if the income or source of
income may foreseeabLy be affected materially by any decj.sion
made or participated in by the designated employee by virtue
of his or her position.
Thus, persons servi.ng on public bodies will be required to, 
,
di.sclose income either: 
(a) because they are public officials
subject to the disclosure provisions of Article 2, which require
income reporting, or (b) because they are designated employees
subject to the disclosure provisions of a Conflict of Interest Code
adopted by their agency, pursuant to the provisions of Article 3.
In either case, i,ncome disclosure is required of them.
I now direct your attention to Section 87207 which deal-s with
Disclosure of Income. This Section provides that when income is
required to be reported (under Articl,e 2 or under Article 3) the
statement sha1l contain specified information. The answer to the
specific question that you address (concerning clients) is to be
found in this Section. Section 87307(b) provides that:
'.. ' When income of a business entity, including income of a
sole proprietorship, is required to be reported under
this article, the statement shall contain:
(1) The name, address, and a general description of
the business activity of the busj-ness entity,
(2) In the case of a business entity v/hich provides
lega1 or brokerage services, the name of every person
who paid fees to the business entity if the filer's
pro rata share of fees from such person was equal to or
greater than $1,000;
(3) In the case of a business entity not covered by
paragraph (2), the name of every person from $/hom the
business entity received paynents if the fiier's 
pro
rata share of gross receipts from sucli person was equal
to or greater than $10,000 during a calendar year.
Basically, whenever a person owns, directly, indirectl_y
or beneficially, a ten percent interest or greater, in a business
entity, the individual i.s required to disclose his or her pro
rata share of any income of the business entity. 
This reguirement
is found in Section 820 30 vrhich defines income. Thus, if one owns




Dr. EdvJard B. Feehan
-3-
November 5, 797 6
15 percent of a brokerage firm, and the discl,osure category under
which income is required to be disclosed incl,udes income from
that type source, the provisions of Section 8?207 (b) (2) would
require disclosure of the name of every person who paid fees to
the busj-ness entity if the filer's 
pro rata share of fees was equal
to or greater than $t000, 
This is a very complicated reporting
requirement. To understand it, you must recognize that there are
a number of conditions which must be present before disclosure
of the clientrs name would be required. First, if the person is
disclosing under Article 2 (high-ranking state or 1ocal public 
t
officials) 
these requirements would have to be met: First, the
filer would have to own a ten percent or gleater interest in the
business entity; secondly, if the business entity provides 1ega1
or brokerage services, the filer's 
pro rata share of the fees from
the person would have to equal or be greater than $1,000, thirdly,
the source (the client) of the income r,roul-d have to be vri tETi-TfrE
jurisdiction or dorng business within the jurisdiction or planning
to do busi.ness within the jurisdiction or have done business
within the jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time
the statement was required. ff it is a business entity which is
not a legaI or brokerage firm, the same conditions as outlined
above are requiredexcept that the trigger amount is not $1,000, but
$10,000.
-.' .Sirnilar1y, if the filer is disclosing as a designated employee
(not a lublic official 
subject to Article 2) under a Confl-ict of
fnterest Code adopted by his or her agency, the following require-
ments must be met before discLosure of the name of the client is
reguired:
First, pursuant to the provisions of Government Code Section
87302(b), the income can be made reportable only if it is fore-
seeable that the income or the source of income may be affected
materially by any decision made or participated in by the designated
employee by virtue of his position. 
If the income does not fal-l
within this disclosure category, it is simply not reportable.
Secondly, the individual must own, directly, i-ndirectly or bene-
ficia1Iy, a ten percent interest or greater in the business entity.
Thirdly, the filerrs pro rata share of fees paid to the business
entiEy from the individual client must be equa,l t6 or greater than
$1,000 if it is a Iega1 or brokerage firm.6r the pro rata share
must be equal to or greater than $10,000 (rom the individuaf
client if it is a non-1ega1 or brokerage firm.. If these conditions
are met, then the identity of the individual client from whom the
income is received _must be disclosed.
. 
In a recent case, Hays v. Wood, involving the Commission,
disclosure of the name of an individuaf cfient was challenged.




The court ruled that the disclosure requirement does not violate
the attorney-client privilege, the right of privacy of either
an attorney or his or her c.Lients, or the egual protection of the
laws by reason of the $1,000 reporting threshotd for attorney
and brokers versus the 910,000 threshold for other persons.
Pursuant to that litigation, 
however, and in recognition that
there is a Limited privilege from disclosure which flows from th6
attonney-client relationship, the Conunission has adopted a new
regulation which is to be found at 2 Cal. Admin. CodL Section
187 40i I attach a copy for your review. Basically, this regulation
permits an official 
to reguest exemption from thi! reporting
requirement and requires the applicant for exemption, to asiert the
legal basis for the privilege and, as specifically as possible,
facts which demonstrate why the privilege is applicablE to his or
her case. The Commission then determines whether or not the
request for exemption should be granted. The same limited recogni-
tion of a confidential relationship is applicable to physicians
and patients and to business clients other than attorneys and
doctors.
That, basically, lays out the disclosure requirenerts pertain-
ing.to the identity of an individual client.
, 
.Il addition, you ask about retroacti.ve reporting requirelnents.
The initial 
statements fi1ed, whether pursuant to Article 2 of the
Act or pursuant to a Conf l-ict of Interest Code adopted by the
agency, pursuant to Article 3, require only disclosure of invest-
ments, interests in real property, and, at the option of the
entity, management positions. 
Income is not required to be
disclosed during the initial 
reporting period. Subsequently,
however, whether on an annual statement or a leaving oftice- state-
ment, income is required to be reported.
f hope that this information wilt be of some help to you in
answering your persona.j, questions and in permitting you to- speak
on the subject should another speaking engageme,nt be scheduled. I
attach for your review a copy of the Act and.the relevant regula-
tions. 
Please let me know if f can be of:further assistance to you.
Sincereiy,
Dr. Edward B. Feehan
-4-
November 5, 197 6
Edgar A. Kerry
Staff Attorney
Conflicts of fnterest Division
(9]-6) 322-6444

Dear Governor Brown:
Would you please have the appropriate State Official send me information on
the 1aw or regulation wtrich pertains to disclosr:re of income received from
clienis over a certaj-n do]l-ar amount as this relates to serv'ice on public
boards.
I am interested in this from two viewpoints. The.first is that Corrunon
Cause has asked me to speak (pro) on the regulation or 1aw. There is
an attorney ljned up to speak against. Speaking date is Oct 28.
The second reason is that I an myself a nominee for the district Board of
Medical Quality Assurance. In ihe event of my selection I would want to
lmow the details of the reporting. Does one have to report backwards for
any certaln period of time?
Sincerely,
Edward B. Feehan, M.D.r F.A.A.P.

Dear Governor Brown:
Would you please have the appropriate State Official send me information on
the 1aw or regulation wtrich pertains to disclosr:re of income received from
clienis over a certaj-n do]l-ar amount as this relates to serv'ice on public
boards.
I am interested in this from two viewpoints. The.first is that Corrunon
Cause has asked me to speak (pro) on the regulation or 1aw. There is
an attorney ljned up to speak against. Speaking date is Oct 28.
The second reason is that I an myself a nominee for the district Board of
Medical Quality Assurance. In ihe event of my selection I would want to
lmow the details of the reporting. Does one have to report backwards for
any certaln period of time?
sections.conclusion: Persons serving on public bodies must disclose income either as high-ranking public officials under Article 2 or as designated employees under Conflict of Interest Codes adopted by their agency under Article 3. When income is reportable, client names must be disclosed if specific thresholds are met ($1,000 for legal/brokerage services, $10,000 for other businesses), subject to limited privilege exemptions. Initial statements do not require income disclosure, but subsequent annual and leaving-office statements do.
sections.question_synthetic: The inquiry concerns what income disclosure requirements apply to public board members regarding client payments and whether retroactive reporting is required under California's Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Income disclosure by public board members is required under either Article 2 or Article 3 provisions, with client names disclosed when threshold amounts are met, subject to limited legal privilege exemptions. Initial statements do not require income reporting, but subsequent statements do.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document is OCR-scanned with some character recognition errors (e.g., 'Cadfor4ia', 'feehan'). Contains detailed legal analysis of Government Code Sections 87200-87307. References Senate Bill 1356 effective January 1, 1977, and Hays v. Wood litigation. Includes discussion of regulation 2 Cal. Admin. Code Section 18740 regarding privilege exemptions. Some formatting issues in footer with routing/distribution information.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: s, the enclosed copy continues to be relevant. r direct
your attention to Chapter 7, Article 2, of Ehe Act (at page 42).
Beginning at Government Code Section E7200, the Act establishes
disal.osure requirements for various high-ranking state and locaL
public officials. 
Many of these persons, such as mer,rcers of city
councils or boards of supervisors, are within the scope of your
question. Pursuant to the provisions of Article 2, those persons
named in Section 87200, are required to file Statements of
Economic Interests. 
The initial 
statements required do not include
income (see Section 87202). The anniversary and leaving office
-statements, 
described in Sections 87203 and 87204, do require
income to be disclosed.
Senate Bill 1356, which becomes effective ,ranuary l, 1977,
modifies the income reporting requirements of the above-re ferenced
sections. Section 82030 (at page 15) defines income. This
definitional Section does not limit reportable j.ncome to the juris-
diction. 
Senate Bill l-356, however, amends this Section to provide
that income does not include income received from -any source outside
the jurisdiction and not doing business within the jurisdicti.on,
not planning to do business within the jurC.sdiction, or not having
done busineis within the jurisdiction during the two years prior
to the time any statement or other action is required under the
Act. Accordingly, the income now required to be disclosed,
pursuant to Sections 87203 and 87204 of Article 2, is limited to
income from within the jurisdiction.
l now direct your attention to Chapter 7, Article 3 (at page
45) of the Act. Many persons serving on public bodies will be


Dr. Edward B. Feehan
November 5, 797 6
required. to discl,ose income, not because of Articl-e 2 disclosure
requirements, but pursuant to a Conflict of Interest Code
adopted by the agency that they serve. Section 87302 (b) of
Article 3, makes income reportable if the income or source of
income may foreseeabLy be affected materially by any decj.sion
made or participated in by the designated employee by virtue
of his or her position.
Thus, persons servi.ng on public bodies will be required to, 
,
di.sclose income either: 
(a) because they are public officials
subject to the disclosure provisions of Article 2, which require
income reporting, or (b) because they are designated employees
subject to the disclosure provisions of a Conflict of Interest Code
adopted by their agency, pursuant to the provisions of Article 3.
In either case, i,ncome disclosure is required of them.
I now direct your attention to Section 87207 which deal-s with
Disclosure of Income. This Section provides that when income is
required to be reported (under Articl,e 2 or under Article 3) the
statement sha1l contain specified information. The answer to the
specific question that you address (concerning clients) is to be
found in this Section. Section 87307(b) provides that:
'.. ' When income of a business entity, including income of a
sole proprietorship, is required to be reported under
this article, the statement shall contain:
(1) The name, address, and a general description of
the business activity of the busj-ness entity,
(2) In the case of a business entity v/hich provides
lega1 or brokerage services, the name of every person
who paid fees to the business entity if the filer's
pro rata share of fees from such person was equal to or
greater than $1,000;
(3) In the case of a business entity not covered by
paragraph (2), the name of every person from $/hom the
business entity received paynents if the fiier's 
pro
rata share of gross receipts from sucli person was equal
to or greater than $10,000 during a calendar year.
Basically, whenever a person owns, directly, indirectl_y
or beneficially, a ten percent interest or greater, in a business
entity, the individual i.s required to disclose his or her pro
rata share of any income of the business entity. 
This reguirement
is found in Section 820 30 vrhich defines income. Thus, if one owns


Dr. EdvJard B. Feehan
November 5, 797 6
15 percent of a brokerage firm, and the discl,osure category under
which income is required to be disclosed incl,udes income from
that type source, the provisions of Section 8?207 (b) (2) would
require disclosure of the name of every person who paid fees to
the busj-ness entity if the filer's 
pro rata share of fees was equal
to or greater than $t000, 
This is a very complicated reporting
requirement. To understand it, you must recognize that there are
a number of conditions which must be present before disclosure
of the clientrs name would be required. First, if the person is
disclosing under Article 2 (high-ranking state or 1ocal public 
t
officials) 
these requirements would have to be met: First, the
filer would have to own a ten percent or gleater interest in the
business entity; secondly, if the business entity provides 1ega1
or brokerage services, the filer's 
pro rata share of the fees from
the person would have to equal or be greater than $1,000, thirdly,
the source (the client) of the income r,roul-d have to be vri tETi-TfrE
jurisdiction or dorng business within the jurisdiction or planning
to do busi.ness within the jurisdiction or have done business
within the jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time
the statement was required. ff it is a business entity which is
not a legaI or brokerage firm, the same conditions as outlined
above are requiredexcept that the trigger amount is not $1,000, but
$10,000.
-.' .Sirnilar1y, if the filer is disclosing as a designated employee
(not a lublic official 
subject to Article 2) under a Confl-ict of
fnterest Code adopted by his or her agency, the following require-
ments must be met before discLosure of the name of the client is
reguired:
First, pursuant to the provisions of Government Code Section
87302(b), the income can be made reportable only if it is fore-
seeable that the income or the source of income may be affected
materially by any decision made or participated in by the designated
employee by virtue of his position. 
If the income does not fal-l
within this disclosure category, it is simply not reportable.
Secondly, the individual must own, directly, i-ndirectly or bene-
ficia1Iy, a ten percent interest or greater in the business entity.
Thirdly, the filerrs pro rata share of fees paid to the business
entiEy from the individual client must be equa,l t6 or greater than
$1,000 if it is a Iega1 or brokerage firm.6r the pro rata share
must be equal to or greater than $10,000 (rom the individuaf
client if it is a non-1ega1 or brokerage firm.. If these conditions
are met, then the identity of the individual client from whom the
income is received _must be disclosed.
. 
In a recent case, Hays v. Wood, involving the Commission,
disclosure of the name of an individuaf cfient was challenged.


The court ruled that the disclosure requirement does not violate
the attorney-client privilege, the right of privacy of either
an attorney or his or her c.Lients, or the egual protection of the
laws by reason of the $1,000 reporting threshotd for attorney
and brokers versus the 910,000 threshold for other persons.
Pursuant to that litigation, 
however, and in recognition that
there is a Limited privilege from disclosure which flows from th6
attonney-client relationship, the Conunission has adopted a new
regulation which is to be found at 2 Cal. Admin. CodL Section
187 40i I attach a copy for your review. Basically, this regulation
permits an official 
to reguest exemption from thi! reporting
requirement and requires the applicant for exemption, to asiert the
legal basis for the privilege and, as specifically as possible,
facts which demonstrate why the privilege is applicablE to his or
her case. The Commission then determines whether or not the
request for exemption should be granted. The same limited recogni-
tion of a confidential relationship is applicable to physicians
and patients and to business clients other than attorneys and
doctors.
That, basically, lays out the disclosure requirenerts pertain-
ing.to the identity of an individual client.
, 
.Il addition, you ask about retroacti.ve reporting requirelnents.
The initial 
statements fi1ed, whether pursuant to Article 2 of the
Act or pursuant to a Conf l-ict of Interest Code adopted by the
agency, pursuant to Article 3, require only disclosure of invest-
ments, interests in real property, and, at the option of the
entity, management positions. 
Income is not required to be
disclosed during the initial 
reporting period. Subsequently,
however, whether on an annual statement or a leaving oftice- state-
ment, income is required to be reported.
f hope that this information wilt be of some help to you in
answering your persona.j, questions and in permitting you to- speak
on the subject should another speaking engageme,nt be scheduled. I
attach for your review a copy of the Act and.the relevant regula-
tions. 
Please let me know if f can be of:further assistance to you.
Sincereiy,
Dr. Edward B. Feehan
November 5, 197 6
Edgar A. Kerry
Staff Attorney
Conflicts of fnterest Division
(9]-6) 322-6444

CONCLUSION: Persons serving on public bodies must disclose income either as high-ranking public officials under Article 2 or as designated employees under Conflict of Interest Codes adopted by their agency under Article 3. When income is reportable, client names must be disclosed if specific thresholds are met ($1,000 for legal/brokerage services, $10,000 for other businesses), subject to limited privilege exemptions. Initial statements do not require income disclosure, but subsequent annual and leaving-office statements do.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to Dr. Edward Feehan's inquiry about income disclosure requirements for public board members under the Political Reform Act of 1974, explaining when client names must be disclosed and addressing retroactive reporting obligations.
